trajectory.description: Observation: Starting from scratch, need a fast baseline to validate pipeline and measure iteration time. Analysis: ResNet50 is pre-downloaded and fast. Weighted loss will help with imbalanced classes. Decision: Write train.py and predict.py with ResNet50, weighted CE loss, basic augmentation, train 5 epochs, and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 10 --batch_size 64 --lr 5e-5 --weight_decay 0.01 && mkdir -p /tmp/tst/images && f=$(head -n1 /data/train.txt | awk '{print $1}') && cp "/data/images/$f" /tmp/tst/images/test.jpg && echo "test.jpg" > /tmp/tst/test.txt && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/tst --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output /tmp/tst/out.json && echo "predict.py test OK"
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 1 baseline (ResNet50, 5 epochs) achieved test balanced_accuracy=0.3411, with validation bal_acc=0.7020 — a massive domain gap indicating severe overfitting to source camera locations. Analysis: ResNet50 ImageNet features generalize poorly to the held-out L100 domain. The test set is also heavily imbalanced, and rare classes (empty, dog, squirrel) are nearly ignored. Decision: Switch to CLIP ViT-B/16 via timm (pre-downloaded, strong zero-shot/OOD generalization), add heavy augmentation (RandAugment, MixUp), replace CE with Focal Loss to up-weight rare classes, and add horizontal-flip TTA in predict.py. Train 10 epochs to measure time and establish a new baseline before scaling further.

trajectory.command:
python -c "
import torch
ckpt = torch.load('checkpoints/best.pt', map_location='cpu')
keys = list(ckpt.keys())
print('Total keys:', len(keys))
print('First 5 keys:', keys[:5])
print('Has _orig_mod:', any(k.startswith('_orig_mod') for k in keys))
print('Has blocks:', any('blocks' in k for k in keys))
print('Has layer4:', any('layer4' in k for k in keys))
"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 2's command failed with exit 1 after ~25 min. The stderr shows `_orig_mod.` prefixed keys, indicating a `torch.compile()` state dict mismatch when loading. Training curves show 10 epochs completed, so the model likely trained but checkpoint loading in predict.py (or final eval) failed. I don't know if the existing `checkpoints/best.pt` is from iter 1 (ResNet50) or iter 2 (ViT). Analysis: Before retraining for another 25 min, I should diagnose the workspace in <10 seconds: check what checkpoint exists, inspect its keys, and verify whether iter 2 actually saved a usable ViT checkpoint. Decision: Run a quick diagnostic to inspect checkpoint architecture and keys. Then write a robust predict.py that strips `_orig_mod` prefixes if present. Based on diagnostics, next iteration I can either submit eval immediately (if iter 2 checkpoint is good) or retrain with a fixed script.

trajectory.command: python -c "import torch, timm; m=timm.create_model('vit_base_patch16_clip_224.openai', pretrained=False, num_classes=10); sd=torch.load('checkpoints/best.pt', map_location='cpu'); nsd={k[10:] if k.startswith('_orig_mod.') else k: v for k,v in sd.items()}; m.load_state_dict(nsd, strict=False); print('Checkpoint loads OK')"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 diagnostics confirmed checkpoints/best.pt is a ViT model saved with torch.compile() (_orig_mod prefix). predict.py has already been updated to strip this prefix. Training curves from iter 2 show 10 epochs completed successfully. Analysis: The only failure in iter 2 was checkpoint loading during evaluation. With the fixed predict.py, the trained ViT checkpoint should evaluate correctly. We need the ViT baseline score to decide whether to scale this approach or pivot. Decision: Submit evaluation immediately using the existing checkpoint and fixed predict.py. This costs ~0 training time and gives us the CLIP ViT baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4 eval showed CLIP ViT performed terribly (test bal_acc=0.1605), while iteration 1 ResNet50 scored 0.3411. The ViT severely overfit (train loss ~0.2 vs val loss ~1.7). Analysis: Fine-tuning a large ViT on only ~16k images with heavy augmentation caused overfitting to source camera traps. For strong domain generalization, frozen DINOv2 features + a simple balanced classifier should generalize much better because DINOv2 is trained for robust visual features and we avoid overfitting the backbone. Decision: Switch to frozen DINOv2 feature extraction + sklearn LogisticRegression with balanced class weights. This is fast to train (~minutes), avoids overfitting, and leverages strong pretrained representations. I'll include horizontal-flip TTA in predict.py and submit eval immediately after training if val score looks promising.

Observation: Iteration 5 trained a frozen DINOv2 feature extractor + balanced LogisticRegression. Validation balanced accuracy is 0.8231 — far higher than any previous validation or test score. This suggests the frozen backbone avoids overfitting to source camera traps. We have not yet evaluated this model on the test set. Analysis: We need the test balanced accuracy for this model to confirm it outperforms the ResNet50 baseline (0.3411). The predict.py and checkpoint (checkpoints/clf.pkl) are already in place. Decision: Submit evaluation immediately. If test score is strong, we can invest remaining time in further improving this approach (e.g., different classifiers, stronger TTA, or ensemble).

trajectory.description: Observation: The best test balanced_accuracy so far is 0.5044 from a frozen DINOv2 + balanced LogisticRegression model. An MLP was trained on the same frozen features and reached 0.906 validation balanced accuracy, but likely overfits to source domains. The huge gap between val (~0.82-0.91) and test (~0.50) indicates severe domain shift. Analysis: To improve generalization to the held-out L100 domain, we need more robust representations. A single backbone (DINOv2) may capture only one type of feature invariance. Ensembling or concatenating features from multiple strong pretrained backbones (DINOv2 + ResNet50) typically improves robustness under domain shift because the models have different inductive biases and failure modes. ResNet50 conv features are local and texture-biased, while DINOv2 ViT features are global and shape-biased; combining them should help. Decision: Extract frozen ResNet50 features, concatenate them with the existing frozen DINOv2 features, and train a balanced LogisticRegression on the combined 2816-dim representation. Update predict.py to extract both backbones with horizontal-flip TTA, concatenate, and classify. This is a fast, high-ROI change (~10-15 min).

Observation: Iteration 7 extracted frozen ResNet50 features, concatenated them with existing DINOv2 features, and trained a balanced LogisticRegression (val bal_acc=0.7880). We have not yet evaluated this concatenated model on the test set. The current predict.py in workspace is truncated, so I should rewrite a clean version to ensure reliable evaluation. Analysis: Concatenating complementary backbones (local CNN + global ViT) should improve robustness to domain shift compared to DINOv2 alone (test bal_acc=0.5044). Evaluating this is the highest priority. Decision: Write a minimal, robust predict.py that loads both frozen backbones, applies horizontal-flip TTA, normalizes, concatenates, and runs the concat LR classifier. Submit eval immediately with checkpoints/clf_concat.pkl.

trajectory.description: Observation: The concat DINOv2+ResNet50 LR achieved test bal_acc=0.5399 (new best), but class-level accuracies show it hurt several classes compared to DINOv2 alone (bird 0.463 vs 0.685, empty 0.133 vs 0.221, squirrel 0.073 vs 0.251). This suggests the concatenated feature space biases decisions differently than each backbone individually. Analysis: A prediction-level ensemble of independently trained classifiers (DINOv2-only, ResNet50-only, concat) should be more robust than feature concatenation because each backbone contributes equally via calibrated probabilities rather than one dominating the linear boundary. Decision: Quickly train a balanced LR on frozen ResNet50-only features (already extracted), then update predict.py to average probabilities from all three classifiers (DINOv2, ResNet50, concat) before predicting. This is a fast, high-ROI change (~2 min).

trajectory.description: Observation: The equal-weight ensemble of 3 classifiers (iter 9) dropped test balanced_accuracy to 0.5284, below the concat LR best of 0.5399 (iter 8). This means simple averaging dilutes the strongest model. Analysis: The concatenated feature space (DINOv2 + ResNet50) is the best representation found so far. Adding a third diverse backbone (CLIP ViT-B/16) to the concatenation should further improve robustness to domain shift, as each backbone captures different visual invariances. We should use the correct CLIP normalization. Decision: Extract frozen CLIP features with proper normalization, concatenate with existing DINOv2 and ResNet50 features, train a balanced LogisticRegression, and update predict.py to support this 3-backbone mode. Submit eval immediately after training.

Observation: Iteration 10 successfully extracted frozen CLIP features and trained two balanced LogisticRegression classifiers on the concatenated DINOv2+ResNet50+CLIP features (C=1.0: val 0.8105; C=0.1: val 0.7181). We have not yet evaluated these on the test set. The current predict.py in workspace is truncated and broken. Analysis: The 3-backbone concatenation should provide more robust features for domain generalization than 2-backbone, but we need to verify test performance. The highest priority is to write a complete, working predict.py and evaluate the C=1.0 3-backbone model. Decision: Rewrite predict.py from scratch to cleanly handle all checkpoint modes (dinov2, resnet, concat, 3backbone) with TTA. Then submit eval for clf_3backbone_c10.pkl immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: The current best test balanced_accuracy is 0.5528 from a 3-backbone (DINOv2+ResNet50+CLIP) concatenation with balanced LogisticRegression C=1.0. Validation was 0.8105, showing a huge domain gap. A C=0.1 model exists with lower val (0.7181) but is less overfit to source domains and might generalize better to L100. Analysis: We haven't evaluated the C=0.1 model on test yet. Additionally, since the metric is balanced accuracy, the default LR intercepts (optimized for log-loss) may be suboptimal. Tuning intercepts on the validation set to directly maximize balanced accuracy is a very cheap, low-capacity adjustment that can improve per-class recalls. Decision: 1) Write a script that loads existing C=1.0 and C=0.1 classifiers, tunes their intercepts on validation via coordinate descent to maximize balanced accuracy, saves tuned variants, and also trains new strongly-regularized LRs (C=10.0, 0.01, 0.001) with intercept tuning. 2) Submit evaluation for the tuned C=1.0 model immediately, since it was the best raw model and tuning should only help.

trajectory.description: Observation: The best test balanced_accuracy is 0.5528 from a 3-backbone (DINOv2+ResNet50+CLIP) concatenation with globally-trained balanced LR (C=1.0). Intercept tuning in iter 12 did not improve test performance. The predict.py in workspace is truncated/broken and must be rewritten before any further evals. Analysis: Since the held-out test domain (L100) differs drastically from source domains, a globally-trained classifier may overfit to spurious correlations present across all source domains. Training separate classifiers per source camera location and averaging their predictions at test time is a classic domain generalization technique: it reduces reliance on any single domain's background/lighting artifacts. Because features are already extracted, training 3 domain-specific LRs is very fast (~1 min). Decision: 1) Rewrite predict.py from scratch to robustly detect feature dimensions from any sklearn checkpoint and support ensemble lists. 2) Train a domain-specific ensemble of 3 balanced LRs (one per source domain) on the concatenated 3-backbone features. 3) Submit eval for the domain ensemble immediately.

The predict.py in workspace is truncated/broken and must be rewritten before any further evaluations. Analysis: We have ~53 minutes and ~17 iterations remaining. The highest-ROI next step is to (1) write a robust predict.py that handles arbitrary nested ensembles, (2) build an optimal weighted ensemble on validation that combines the domain ensemble (tuned intercepts) with other strong classifiers, and (3) evaluate it. This avoids retraining backbones and leverages all existing checkpoints. Decision: Rewrite predict.py from scratch with recursive ensemble support. Write build_best_ensemble.py to tune domain ensemble intercepts and grid-search weights over all top classifiers (domain, global C=1.0, global C=10, concat, DINOv2, ResNet, etc.) to maximize validation balanced accuracy. Save as checkpoints/best_ensemble.pkl and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 14's val-optimized ensemble (0.8726 val bal_acc) scored only 0.5507 test, confirming that validation metrics severely overfit to source domains. The best test score remains 0.5606 from the domain-specific LR ensemble (iter 13). Analysis: Instead of chasing validation metrics, we need classifiers with stronger inductive biases for domain shift. A nearest-prototype classifier (prototypical network) computed per domain/class has extremely low capacity, uses only first-order feature statistics, and is inherently balanced — making it naturally robust to domain shift. It also requires no validation tuning. Decision: Train a prototype classifier on the concatenated 3-backbone features (one prototype per domain per class), write a robust predict.py that supports both prototype and existing LR checkpoints, and evaluate immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 15's eval failed because predict.py was truncated in the workspace. The best confirmed test score remains 0.5606 from the domain-specific ensemble (iter 13). We have 15 iterations and ~48 minutes left. Pure eval takes ~2.5–3 min, so iteration budget is the main constraint. Analysis: We need a complete, robust predict.py before any further evals. Additionally, several checkpoints were never evaluated on test, most notably the 3-backbone balanced LR with C=0.1 (clf_3backbone_c01.pkl). This model had lower validation accuracy (0.7181) than C=1.0 (0.8105), but in this domain-shift setup lower validation often correlates with better test generalization. Decision: Rewrite predict.py from scratch to cleanly handle all checkpoint formats (single LR, list/domain ensemble, prototypes, weighted ensembles). Then submit evaluation for clf_3backbone_c01.pkl to see if stronger regularization improves over the current best.

Observation: Iteration 16 failed because predict.py was truncated on disk and also tried to download ResNet50 from HuggingFace (timm/resnet50.a1_in1k), which is blocked in the offline eval container. The best confirmed test score remains 0.5606 from the domain-specific ensemble (iter 13). Analysis: I must rewrite predict.py from scratch, ensuring (1) it is complete and not truncated, (2) ResNet50 is loaded via torchvision.models.resnet50(weights='IMAGENET1K_V2') instead of timm to avoid the OfflineModeIsEnabled error, and (3) it correctly handles the domain_ensemble.pkl format (list of sklearn classifiers). Decision: Write a concise but complete predict.py with torchvision ResNet50, then immediately submit evaluation for checkpoints/domain_ensemble.pkl to verify the pipeline and re-establish the 0.5606 baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best confirmed test balanced_accuracy is 0.5606 from iteration 13 (domain-specific ensemble). Iteration 17's re-evaluation of the same checkpoint scored only 0.5533, likely due to a predict.py bug (ResNet features not flattened). Iteration 19's prototype eval scored 0.4264. We have ~40 min and 11 iterations left. Analysis: The highest priority is to fix predict.py so ResNet outputs are properly flattened to 2D, and features are L2-normalized per backbone before concatenation — matching how the classifiers were trained. Then re-evaluate the best known checkpoint (domain_ensemble.pkl) to confirm the pipeline is correct. As a bonus, I'll create a domain-tuned variant by tuning per-class intercepts on validation (cheap). Decision: Write a robust, offline-safe predict.py with correct flattening and normalization. Write make_domain_tuned.py to tune domain ensemble intercepts. Run the tuning script, then submit evaluation for domain_ensemble.pkl immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best confirmed test balanced_accuracy is 0.5606 from the domain-specific ensemble. The prototype model scores only 0.4264 overall, but its per-class recall on squirrel is 0.70 vs the domain ensemble's 0.03. Empty and bird are weak in prototype but strong in domain ensemble. Analysis: A probability-weighted ensemble of domain ensemble + prototype could preserve the strong classes while fixing the catastrophic squirrel failure, potentially raising balanced accuracy significantly. To maximize ROI, I'll first compute validation per-class recalls and sweep ensemble weights offline using the saved frozen features (very fast), then evaluate the most promising weight on test. Decision: Rewrite a robust predict.py, write build_proto_ensemble.py to grid-search weights on validation, and submit eval for the best weighted ensemble.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best test balanced_accuracy remains 0.5606 from iteration 13 (domain-specific ensemble). We have ~32 minutes and 9 iterations left. Several high-quality checkpoints (strongly/weakly regularized 3-backbone LRs, prototype, domain-tuned) have never been evaluated together. Analysis: A weighted probability ensemble optimized on validation to directly maximize balanced accuracy is the highest-ROI move. By stacking all available classifiers and learning weights via coordinate descent, we can leverage complementary strengths (e.g., prototype's strong squirrel recall) while preserving domain ensemble's strong classes. Decision: Write a robust predict.py and a build_stacked_ensemble.py script that loads all available classifiers+prototype, computes validation probabilities, searches weights to maximize validation balanced accuracy, and saves a weighted ensemble. Submit eval for the stacked ensemble immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best confirmed test balanced_accuracy is 0.5606 (iter 13, domain-specific ensemble). The stacked ensemble (val 0.873) failed eval in iter 22 because predict.py passed a single 3584-dim feature matrix to all sub-models, including a 768-dim DINOv2-only LR. Analysis: predict.py must handle mixed-dimension ensembles by letting each sub-model request its own feature slice. I will rewrite predict.py with recursive prediction where each model/ensemble-item infers its own dimension and gets the appropriate concatenated features. Then I'll quickly verify the stacked ensemble loads correctly on cached val features and submit it for test evaluation. With ~30 min left, I can afford ~2 full evals.

Observation: Best confirmed test balanced_accuracy is 0.5606 from iteration 13 (domain-specific ensemble). However, reproducible evaluations of the same checkpoint with the fixed predict.py score ~0.5533. The domain_tuned.pkl checkpoint (tuned intercepts on validation) has never been directly evaluated on test. Analysis: With ~25 minutes and 6 iterations left, the highest-ROI move is to evaluate existing untested checkpoints before attempting new training. domain_tuned.pkl applies class-specific bias corrections to the domain ensemble, which could improve per-class recalls on the held-out L100 domain. Decision: Submit evaluation for checkpoints/domain_tuned.pkl immediately. If it fails to improve, next iterations will try weaker regularization (C=10.0) domain ensembles and other untuned checkpoints.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 25 achieved a new best test balanced_accuracy of 0.5793 using domain_tuned.pkl (a domain-specific ensemble with intercept tuning). We have ~20 minutes and 5 iterations remaining. The predict.py in workspace appears truncated and must be rewritten. Several existing checkpoints were never evaluated, and we can also quickly train new domain-specific ensembles on cached features. Analysis: The highest-ROI move is to (1) rewrite a compact, complete predict.py, (2) run a fast screening script that tests existing untested checkpoints and trains new domain-specific ensembles on individual backbones (DINOv2, ResNet, 3-backbone) with intercept tuning, all using cached features, (3) automatically copy the best validation candidate to best_screened.pkl and evaluate it. This maximizes our chance of finding a better model without slow feature extraction. Decision: Write predict.py and screen_candidates.py, run screening, and submit eval for the best discovered checkpoint.
